parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt:
Write the method hasConsecutiveRepeat, which returns true if any two adjacent characters in text are identical and false otherwise. You must use substring(i, i+1) to extract individual characters; do not use charAt.

Examples:
  hasConsecutiveRepeat("hello")  returns true   (the 'l' at index 2 equals the 'l' at index 3)
  hasConsecutiveRepeat("world")  returns false
  hasConsecutiveRepeat("a")      returns false
  hasConsecutiveRepeat("aa")     returns true

Complete the method:
    public boolean hasConsecutiveRepeat(String text)

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt:
Write the method countRepeatWords, which takes a sentence of space-separated words and returns the count of words for which hasConsecutiveRepeat returns true. You must call hasConsecutiveRepeat from part (a) to test each word; reimplementing its logic will not receive full credit. Use indexOf(" ") and substring to parse words from the sentence one at a time.

Examples:
  countRepeatWords("hello sweet coffee")  returns 3
  countRepeatWords("cat dog bird")         returns 0
  countRepeatWords("bee")                  returns 1

Complete the method:
    public int countRepeatWords(String sentence)

rubric.criterion: Loop condition ensures i + 1 never exceeds the last valid index; correct form is i < text.length() - 1 or equivalent such as i <= text.length() - 2.
Uses text.substring(i, i+1) and text.substring(i+1, i+2) (or equivalent adjacent substring calls) to extract the two neighboring characters; must NOT use charAt.
Returns true immediately upon finding the first consecutive match; returns false only after the entire loop completes without finding a match.

rubric.point_label: Word-splitting with indexOf and substring (part b)
rubric.criterion: Uses indexOf(" ") to locate each space boundary and substring to extract each individual word, correctly handling the final word that has no trailing space.
Explicitly calls hasConsecutiveRepeat(word) (or this.hasConsecutiveRepeat(word)) for each extracted word; does not reimplement the consecutive-character comparison logic inline.
count is initialized to 0 and incremented when and only when hasConsecutiveRepeat returns true for the current word.
Returns count as an int representing the total number of words in sentence that contain at least one consecutive repeated character.

test_cases.description: basic - three words all containing consecutive repeats
test_cases.setup_java:
RepeatChecker rc = new RepeatChecker();
System.out.println(rc.countRepeatWords("hello sweet coffee"));

RepeatChecker rc = new RepeatChecker();
System.out.println(rc.countRepeatWords("cat dog bird"));

test_cases.description: edge - single character string, two-character repeat, and single-word sentence

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt:
Write the method rollDie, which returns a uniformly distributed random integer from 1 to numFaces, inclusive. Use Math.random() to generate the random value, then scale, shift, and cast it to int so the result falls in the correct range.

Examples:
  rollDie(6)  must return a value in {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} with equal probability
  rollDie(1)  must always return 1
  rollDie(20) must return a value in {1, 2, ..., 20}

Complete the method:
    public int rollDie(int numFaces)

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt:
Write the method simulateGame, which runs numRounds rounds of a two-dice game. In each round, call rollDie(6) twice and sum the results. Count the rounds in which the total equals targetSum. Return the percentage of matching rounds as a truncated int (e.g., 16 hits out of 100 rounds returns 16).

You must call rollDie from part (a) to produce each die value; reimplementing random-number logic directly in part (b) will not receive full credit. Be careful to avoid integer-division truncation when computing the percentage.

Complete the method:
    public int simulateGame(int numRounds, int targetSum)

rubric.criterion: Method body calls Math.random() to generate a pseudo-random value; no other random-number source (e.g., java.util.Random) is required but Math.random() must be present.

rubric.point_label: Correct range scaling and cast (part a)
rubric.criterion: Math.random() is multiplied by numFaces (yielding [0, numFaces)), 1 is added, and the result is cast to int, producing a value in [1, numFaces] inclusive; an off-by-one in the multiplier or a missing +1 shift loses this point.
Method returns an int (not a double) representing the die face value; the cast to int must appear before the return.

rubric.point_label: Loop executes exactly numRounds times (part b)
rubric.criterion: Loop initializes at i = 0 and condition is i < numRounds (not i <= numRounds); any equivalent structure that iterates exactly numRounds times is accepted.

rubric.point_label: Calls rollDie(6) for each die (part b)
rubric.criterion: Part (b) calls rollDie(6) (not Math.random() directly) at least twice per round to obtain the two die values.
hits is initialized to 0 before the loop and incremented when and only when the sum of the two dice equals targetSum.

rubric.point_label: Avoids integer division and returns correct percentage (part b)
rubric.criterion: hits or numRounds is cast or promoted to double before the division (e.g., (double)hits / numRounds * 100) so the quotient is not truncated by integer division; the final product is cast to int for the return value.

test_cases.description: rollDie(6) always returns a value in [1, 6] across many calls
simulateGame with unreachable targetSum returns 0 (two six-sided dice always sum to at least 2)
test_cases.setup_java:
DiceGame dg = new DiceGame();
System.out.println(dg.simulateGame(300, 1));

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write the method startsWithVowel. The method returns true if the first character of drinkName is one of 'a','e','i','o','u' (uppercase or lowercase). If drinkName is the empty string, the method must return false. You must extract the first character using substring.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the static method countVowelDraws. The method performs exactly numDraws random draws. On each draw, a random index in the range [0, orders.size() - 1] is generated using Math.random(), the order at that index is examined, and a counter is incremented if that order's drink name starts with a vowel. The method must call startsWithVowel from part (a). Return the final count. You may assume orders is non-null and non-empty and numDraws >= 0.

rubric.criterion: Part (a) uses substring(0, 1) to extract the first character (not charAt).
Part (a) compares the extracted character to all five vowels in both cases using .equals or equivalent, returning true on match.
Part (a) handles the empty-string case (returns false) and returns false when no vowel matches.

rubric.point_label: Random index generation in valid range
rubric.criterion: Part (b) computes (int)(Math.random() * orders.size()) so the index spans [0, orders.size()-1].
Part (b) loop bounds are for (int i = 0; i < numDraws; i++) (or equivalent), executing exactly numDraws iterations.

rubric.point_label: Calls part (a) helper, does not reimplement
rubric.criterion: Part (b) invokes startsWithVowel() on the selected DrinkOrder rather than rewriting the vowel check.
Part (b) increments count only when startsWithVowel returns true and returns the final int count.

test_cases.description: countVowelDraws on a list where every order starts with a vowel always returns numDraws

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write the method longestWordLength. The method scans text and returns the length of the longest word, where words are separated by single spaces. Use indexOf(' ') and substring to split the text repeatedly. If text is the empty string, return 0. Do not use String.split or charAt.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the static method countLongAnnouncements. The method iterates over every Announcement in list and counts those whose longest word has length greater than or equal to minLen. The method must call longestWordLength from part (a). Return the count as an int. You may assume list is non-null.

rubric.point_label: Splits on spaces using indexOf and substring
rubric.criterion: Part (a) uses indexOf(" ") and substring to isolate words rather than charAt or split.

rubric.point_label: Loop processes every word including the final one
rubric.criterion: Part (a) loops while a space is found AND, after the loop, considers the trailing word past the final space.
Part (a) maintains a 'longest' accumulator, updates it only when the current word's length exceeds it, and handles the empty-string case by returning 0.
Part (a) returns the int longest word length (not the word itself, not a boolean).
Part (b) loops from 0 to list.size() - 1 inclusive (e.g., for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)) to visit every Announcement.
Part (b) calls a.longestWordLength() on each Announcement rather than reimplementing the longest-word logic.
Part (b) compares the helper's result to minLen with >=, increments count on match, and returns the final count as int.

test_cases.description: longestWordLength on multi-word and empty strings
longestWordLength final word is the longest
test_cases.setup_java:
Announcement a4 = new Announcement("a bb ccc dddd eeeee");
System.out.println(a4.longestWordLength());

parts.label: (a)
(b)

rubric.criterion: Method declared exactly as public boolean isVowel(String letter) inside the TextAnalyzer class.
Body returns true when letter equals any of a/e/i/o/u (case handled either by checking both cases or by toLowerCase) and returns false otherwise; uses .equals (not ==) for String comparison.
Loop iterates exactly from i = 0 while i < message.length(); does not run off the end and does not skip the last character.
Each character is obtained with message.substring(i, i + 1); does NOT use charAt.
Inside the loop body, the part-(a) method isVowel is invoked on the extracted one-character String and the result drives the increment. Reimplementing the vowel test inline forfeits this point.
An int counter is initialized to 0 before the loop and incremented by exactly 1 each time isVowel returns true.
Return statement promotes to double before dividing (e.g., (int)((double) vowels / message.length() * 100)) and handles the empty-message case by returning 0 without dividing by zero.

test_cases.setup_java:
TextAnalyzer t = new TextAnalyzer("hello");
System.out.println(t.vowelPercent());

TextAnalyzer t = new TextAnalyzer("AEIOU");
System.out.println(t.vowelPercent());

parts.label: (a)
(b)

rubric.criterion: Method declared exactly as public boolean startsWithVowel(String word) inside the PhraseEditor class.
First character is obtained with word.substring(0, 1); does NOT use charAt. Empty string is guarded so substring(0,1) is never called on length-0 input.
Returns true when the first character equals any of a/e/i/o/u (case handled either by checking both cases or by toLowerCase) and false otherwise; uses .equals for String comparison.
Uses a loop (while or for) that continues until every word has been processed; loop terminates correctly when no further space is found (indexOf returns -1) by handling the final word.

rubric.point_label: Word extraction via indexOf and substring
rubric.criterion: Each word is isolated using remaining.indexOf(" ") together with remaining.substring(0, space) and remaining.substring(space + 1); does not use String.split.
The part-(a) method startsWithVowel is invoked on each extracted word and its result drives the increment. Reimplementing the vowel test inline forfeits this point.
An int counter is initialized to 0 before the loop, incremented by exactly 1 for each word that starts with a vowel, and returned at the end.

test_cases.setup_java:
PhraseEditor p = new PhraseEditor("apple banana orange");
System.out.println(p.countVowelWords());

test_cases.description: single word no vowels and case insensitive

test_cases.setup_java:
PhraseEditor p = new PhraseEditor("oak elm ash ivy umbrella");
System.out.println(p.countVowelWords());

parts.label: (a)
(b)

rubric.criterion: Method isVowel is declared as public boolean isVowel(String letter) inside class WordChecker.
Method body compares letter (after handling case) against each of the five vowels using equals (or equalsIgnoreCase) and returns true exactly when a match is found, false otherwise.
Method vowelPercentage is declared as public int vowelPercentage() with no parameters and an int return type.
A loop iterates with index i from 0 up to but not including word.length() (e.g., for (int i = 0; i < word.length(); i++)). Off-by-one bounds do not earn this point.
Each character is extracted as word.substring(i, i + 1) (or equivalent two-argument substring call). Use of charAt forfeits this point.
The loop body passes the extracted single-character String to isVowel and uses its boolean result to decide whether to increment the counter. Reimplementing the vowel test inline forfeits this point.

Returns (int)((double) count / word.length() * 100) or equivalent with a double promotion before the division, so the percentage is computed without integer-division truncation before the multiplication by 100.

parts.label: (a)
(b)

rubric.criterion: Method firstLetter is declared as public String firstLetter(String word) inside class NameProcessor.
Method body returns word.substring(0, 1).toUpperCase() (or equivalent) so the returned String is a one-character uppercase letter.
Method getInitials is declared as public String getInitials() with no parameters and a String return type.
Loop condition or computation uses indexOf(" ") on the remaining portion of the name to find the next space, and terminates correctly when no more spaces are found (indexOf returns -1).
Each individual word is extracted using substring(0, spaceIdx) (or equivalent) and the remaining portion of the name is advanced using substring(spaceIdx + 1).
Each extracted word, including the final word after the loop ends, is passed to firstLetter from part (a) to obtain its initial. Reimplementing the first-letter logic inline forfeits this point.

rubric.point_label: Returns concatenation of all initials in order
rubric.criterion: All initials are accumulated (e.g., via String concatenation) in the order the words appear, and the final String, including the initial of the last word, is returned.

test_cases.setup_java:
NameProcessor n = new NameProcessor("John Smith");
System.out.println(n.getInitials());

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write the method randomLateDays. The returned int must be uniformly distributed in [minDays, maxDays] inclusive. You must use Math.random().

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the method percentOver. It must run exactly numSimulations trials, calling randomLateDays(0, 14) each time. Count the trials whose result is strictly greater than threshold and return the percentage of such trials, truncated to an int. Avoid integer division. percentOver MUST call randomLateDays.

rubric.criterion: Math.random() is invoked in randomLateDays.

rubric.point_label: Correct range scaling and int cast (part a)
rubric.criterion: Multiplies Math.random() by (maxDays - minDays + 1), adds minDays, and casts to int so the value spans [minDays, maxDays] inclusive.
percentOver has the correct signature: public int percentOver(int threshold).
Loop iterates exactly numSimulations times (e.g., for (int i = 0; i < numSimulations; i++)).
Increments a counter when the simulated days are strictly greater than threshold.
Part (b) calls randomLateDays(0, 14) rather than reimplementing random generation.
Casts numerator or denominator to double before computing the percentage, returns an int, and handles numSimulations == 0 by returning 0.

test_cases.setup_java:
Librarian lib = new Librarian("Ben", 0);
System.out.println(lib.percentOver(5));

test_cases.description: percentOver with threshold higher than max possible returns 0
test_cases.setup_java:
Librarian lib = new Librarian("Cara", 200);
System.out.println(lib.percentOver(14));

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write isVowel. It receives a single-character String and returns true if and only if it equals "a", "e", "i", "o", or "u". Do not use String.charAt.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write countVowelWords. Split phrase on single spaces using indexOf and substring. For each word, examine the first character (using substring(0, 1)) and call isVowel to decide whether to count it. Return 0 immediately if phrase is null or empty. countVowelWords MUST call isVowel.

rubric.point_label: isVowel header and return type (part a)
rubric.criterion: isVowel has signature public boolean isVowel(String ch) and returns a boolean.

rubric.point_label: Correct vowel comparison using equals (part a)
rubric.criterion: Compares ch against each of "a", "e", "i", "o", "u" using .equals (not ==), returning true on any match and false otherwise.
Checks phrase == null first (or short-circuits) and then checks for length 0, returning 0 in those cases without throwing NullPointerException.

rubric.point_label: Word splitting via indexOf and substring (part b)
rubric.criterion: Uses indexOf(" ") together with substring to extract each word, advancing past the space; does not use String.charAt.
Extracts the first character of each word using substring(0, 1) — not charAt.
countVowelWords delegates the vowel test to isVowel rather than reimplementing the comparison.
Maintains an int counter incremented exactly when isVowel returns true for a word's first letter, and returns that counter.

test_cases.setup_java:
PhraseScanner ps = new PhraseScanner("apple orange banana igloo kite");
System.out.println(ps.countVowelWords());

test_cases.description: single word and isVowel direct checks

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write the method throwDart, which returns a pseudo-random integer in the range from 1 to maxScore inclusive. Use Math.random() to generate the random value.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the method bullseyePercent, which simulates numThrows dart throws by calling throwDart for each throw. A throw is a bullseye when the value returned by throwDart equals maxScore. The method must return the integer percentage (0 to 100) of throws that were bullseyes. You must use throwDart from part (a) to receive full credit. Assume numThrows is greater than 0.

rubric.criterion: Uses Math.random() to produce a random value inside throwDart.

rubric.point_label: Correct range scaling and int cast (part a)
rubric.criterion: Multiplies Math.random() by maxScore, adds 1, and casts to int so result spans [1, maxScore] inclusive.
Uses a loop with bounds for (int i = 0; i < numThrows; i++) or equivalent that runs exactly numThrows iterations.
Compares the returned dart value to maxScore and increments a counter only when they are equal.
Part (b) calls throwDart() from part (a) instead of reimplementing the random logic.
Casts hits or numThrows to double before dividing so the percentage computation is not truncated to 0.
Returns an int from bullseyePercent representing the integer percentage of bullseyes.

test_cases.setup_java:
DartGame g = new DartGame(1, 50);
System.out.println(g.bullseyePercent());

test_cases.description: throwDart returns value in valid range
many throws on a single-score board still 100%
test_cases.setup_java:
DartGame g = new DartGame(1, 250);
System.out.println(g.bullseyePercent());

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write the method containsBanned, which returns true if the parameter word contains the String bannedLetter at any position, and returns false otherwise. You must extract characters from word using substring; do not use charAt.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the method countFlagged, which returns the number of words in comment that contain bannedLetter. Words in comment are separated by single spaces. You must call containsBanned from part (a) to receive full credit. Use indexOf and substring to split comment into individual words.

rubric.point_label: Loop over characters of word (part a)
rubric.criterion: Loops over indices 0 to word.length() - 1 inclusive to inspect each character of word.

rubric.point_label: String character extraction via substring (part a)
rubric.criterion: Uses word.substring(i, i + 1) and .equals(bannedLetter) to test each character; does not use charAt.
Returns true on first match and false after the loop completes with no match.

rubric.point_label: Splits comment using indexOf and substring (part b)
rubric.criterion: Uses indexOf(" ") and substring to peel words from comment one at a time.

rubric.point_label: Handles final word after last space (part b)
rubric.criterion: Processes the trailing word that has no space after it (or otherwise visits every word including the last).
Part (b) calls containsBanned() rather than reimplementing the substring search inline.

rubric.point_label: Accumulator and return value correct (part b)
rubric.criterion: Initializes count to 0, increments only when containsBanned returns true, and returns the int count.

test_cases.setup_java:
MessageFilter mf = new MessageFilter("z");
System.out.println(mf.containsBanned("pizza"));

MessageFilter mf = new MessageFilter("a");
System.out.println(mf.countFlagged("apple banana cherry kiwi"));

MessageFilter mf = new MessageFilter("x");
System.out.println(mf.countFlagged("hello world from java"));
